coredata.dc:description: Biodiesel is a renewable alternative to petroleum diesel that is produced through the chemical reaction of oils and fats derived from plants and animals with short-chain alcohols. Widespread interest has been shown in biodiesel as a possible substitute for fuels derived from fossil fuels. Heterogeneous catalysts can provide a more appealing and environmentally beneficial method for meeting cleaner, sustainable production needs when used in the biodiesel synthesis process. Transesterification is the prevailing method for biodiesel production, wherein the reaction occurs spontaneously but is frequently facilitated by both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Heterogeneous catalysts, such as metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, are chosen over homogeneous catalysts due to their advantages of not requiring special equipment and being easier to remove from the end product. Nevertheless, traditional heterogeneous catalysts possess many drawbacks, such as limited suitability for industrial use. Carbon-based nanoparticles, such as carbon dots (C-dots), have recently been suggested as possible catalysts. C-dots, a nascent discovery, show great potential as a heterogeneous catalyst because of their straightforward and economical synthesis, adaptability, large specific surface area, precise specificity, and exceptional conversion efficiency. In addition, unlike other catalysts, C-dots do not require removal after biodiesel production and can be used as an additive that enhances fuel and engine efficiency while decreasing greenhouse gas emissions. This brief analysis seeks to emphasize the growing significance of C-dots in the process of biodiesel production by transesterification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different types of carbon dots with surface-functionalisation.
Commonly used top-down and bottom-up approaches to C-dot synthesis.

displays the temporal evolution of publication trends related to the applications of C-dots. (A) There are four main uses of C-dots. (B) A number of articles on fluorescence, nanodots, and carbon nanoparticles have been published throughout time. (C) The publication patterns of different applications of C-dots, such as sensing, optical, biomedical, and catalytic applications, have been studied over time.
Illustrates the mechanism by which carbon dots catalyze transesterification.
The merits and demerits of diverse carbon-based nanocatalysts as applied to the production of biodiesel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodiesel is a renewable alternative to petroleum diesel that is produced through the chemical reaction of oils and fats derived from plants and animals with short-chain alcohols. Widespread interest has been shown in biodiesel as a possible substitute for fuels derived from fossil fuels. Heterogeneous catalysts can provide a more appealing and environmentally beneficial method for meeting cleaner, sustainable production needs when used in the biodiesel synthesis process. Transesterification is the prevailing method for biodiesel production, wherein the reaction occurs spontaneously but is frequently facilitated by both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Heterogeneous catalysts, such as metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, are chosen over homogeneous catalysts due to their advantages of not requiring special equipment and being easier to remove from the end product. Nevertheless, traditional heterogeneous catalysts possess many drawbacks, such as limited suitability for industrial use. Carbon-based nanoparticles, such as carbon dots (C-dots), have recently been suggested as possible catalysts. C-dots, a nascent discovery, show great potential as a heterogeneous catalyst because of their straightforward and economical synthesis, adaptability, large specific surface area, precise specificity, and exceptional conversion efficiency. In addition, unlike other catalysts, C-dots do not require removal after biodiesel production and can be used as an additive that enhances fuel and engine efficiency while decreasing greenhouse gas emissions. This brief analysis seeks to emphasize the growing significance of C-dots in the process of biodiesel production by transesterification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Worldwide, the progress of industrial engineering, rapid increase in the number of cars, and excessive use of traditional fossil fuels have collectively contributed to a mounting number of significant environmental problems and energy emergencies on a global scale [1]. Biodiesels have demonstrated their immense value as a renewable energy source since their initial discovery. Biodiesel is typically a blend of long-chain fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs) derived from various sources such as waste cooking oil (WCO), animal fats, vegetable oil, and various other biofeedstocks. This is achieved through the process of transesterification, where triglycerides and methanol are converted into FAMEs, or through esterification, where free fatty acids (FFAs) and methanol are converted into FAMEs [2,3]. These biofuels have demonstrated their ability to improve energy security, boost air quality, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions [4]. Biodiesels possess a diverse array of applications, encompassing the propulsion of cars, the provision of heat, and the generation of electricity [5]. Additionally, they can be utilized for lesser-known purposes, such as the remediation of grease and oil spills, as well as serving as engine lubricants. Consequently, biodiesel is an essential and irreplaceable commodity [6]. Biodiesels are mostly produced via transesterification, a chemical process that involves combining fats and oils obtained from animals and plants with short-chain ethanol to produce alkyl esters [7,8]. Although many synthetic methods are available, transesterification is the most generally employed approach.

Transesterification, unlike other common processes like as micro-emulsification, blending, and pyrolysis, takes place at lower pressures and temperatures [9–11]. It results in the production of pure biofuels that have a chemical composition similar to petrodiesel. Transesterification occurs naturally when exposed to heat and pressure, although it proceeds at a sluggish pace [12]. Therefore, the reaction is frequently facilitated with a catalyst. Over time, various catalysts have been effectively employed to expedite the transesterification process, including magnetic solid catalysts as well as homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts [13–16]. Transesterification is a commonly used process for preparing biodiesel due to its advantages, such as the availability of a wide range of raw materials, less severe conditions for reaction, and higher biodiesel production. Despite its superior yield compared to other synthetic processes, lipase-catalyzed transesterification is hardly employed due to its prolonged reaction time and high related costs [17]. Furthermore, homogenous catalysts, such as potent acids and bases, are not favored because of their numerous restrictions. These factors encompass the need for corrosion-resistant equipment, the difficulty of isolating the catalyst's components from the final product after the reaction is complete, and the existence of unwanted contaminants [18].

In order to address these drawbacks, the majority of transesterification reactions employ heterogeneous catalysts for catalysis. Heterogeneous catalysts, such as metal oxides, including TiO, SrO, and other CaO, are cost-effective and efficient in speeding up transesterification [19,20]. Currently a growing inclination to produce catalysts from biomass resources, which is beneficial for environmental conservation [21]. Heterogeneous catalysts possess inherent characteristics that render them more suitable for transesterification compared to lipases. These properties include superior solubility, specificity, heightened catalytic properties, exceptional thermal stability, and effortless separation from the product, leading to relatively elevated product purity. Recently, scientists have been investigating the possibility of reducing the size of these metals and metal oxide heterogeneous catalysts to the nanoscale.

Research has demonstrated that this enhances their catalytic efficacy and selectivity as a result of an augmentation in specific surface area and surface energy [22]. Despite being the most appropriate among conventional catalysts, nanometals and nano metal oxides still have various drawbacks that adversely affect the efficiency of transesterification. These limitations include time inefficiency, low efficiency, resistance to mass transfer, high catalyst loading, and limited suitability for industrial applications [23]. Moreover, metallic nanocatalysts have a high cost and are susceptible to leaching, hence impeding the widespread commercial manufacturing of biodiesel utilizing these catalysts. One significant drawback of metallic nanocatalysts is their rapid deactivation caused by coke production [24]. This occurs when the nanometal/nanometal oxide combines with the hydrocarbons employed in the reaction, leading to suboptimal biodiesel output. In order to overcome these problems, scientists have suggested the utilization of carbon-based heterogeneous nanocatalysts.

Carbon-based nanocatalysts, similar to metallic ones, exhibit notable characteristics such as a large specific surface area, high catalytic activity, and reactivity [25]. Additionally, they demonstrate exceptional selectivity and specificity, display efficient performance, and effectively minimize reaction time. Carbon-based nanoparticles have demonstrated significant efficacy as heterogeneous catalysts in recent decades [26]. Furthermore, these materials possess exceptional mechanical and support characteristics. They also have surfaces that can be altered with diverse functional groups, allowing for the incorporation of heteroatoms into their structure. This feature greatly enhances their potential for various applications [27–30]. Furthermore, they have a lack of reactivity towards unwanted reactions and show exceptional durability during the process of regeneration and reaction.

Furthermore, they can be acquired in a diverse array of forms and dimensions to suit various chemical reactor arrangements. Carbon-based nanocatalysts, unlike metallic nanocatalysts, have superior resistance to coke production and possess a low affinities for coke [31]. As a result, they do not react with the lengthy hydrocarbon chains present in the fatty acids and monoalkyl esters, enabling them to preserve their catalytic function and increase the yield of biodiesel. Carbon-based nanoparticles exhibit distinctive physicochemical characteristics, such as excellent thermal, electrical conductivity, mechanical stability, and strong light absorption capacity [32,33]. These properties arise from their distinctive electron transfer, structural, optical and characteristics, which also make them exceptional catalysts [34].

Several studies have demonstrated the efficacy of carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanohorns, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanofibres, in rapidly and effectively catalyzing biodiesel generation [35]. Recently, researchers have started investigating the feasibility of carbon dots (C-dots). This brief study focuses on highlighting the growing significance of C-dots in biodiesel production and providing a rationale for their possible utilization and integration on a broad scale. Section two delves into the many categories of C-dots, elucidates their synthesis, heteroatom doping, and functionalization, investigates their optical and electron transmission characteristics, and emphasizes their aptness as an environmentally friendly technology [36]. In the next part, we will discuss the specific characteristics of carbon-based nanomaterials that make them appropriate catalysts for transesterification.

Additionally, we will provide many instances where C-dots have been utilized as catalysts in the manufacture of biodiesel. Section four provides a concise overview of the present condition of this domain and presents our insights on the prospects of this method for transesterification. We provide a concise summary of the primary arguments advocating for the utilization of C-dots in the manufacturing of biodiesel. The papers examined in this review were sourced from various academic research databases, including Google Scholar, Web of Science, Scopus, PubMed, ScienceDirect, and IEEE Xplore. The search was performed utilizing precise keywords, including carbon dot optical properties, heterogeneous catalysts,carbon dots, catalysis, carbon dot catalysis, transesterification, carbon dot electron transfer properties, biodiesel production, green technology, catalytic carbon nanomaterials, and fuel additives. Furthermore, SciVal was employed to visually represent publication trends and determine the level of scientific curiosity in this area of study [37].

This review cited a total of 98 relevant sources. Subsequently, the phrase 'carbon dots in biodiesel production' was employed to locate practical instances where C-dots were utilized as catalysts for transesterification. Five papers were discovered that emphasized the utilization of C-dots as co-catalysts or exclusive catalysts in the synthesis of biodiesel. All examples, except for one published in 2016, were published within the timeframe of 2019 to 2024. This suggests a growing interest in the utilization of C-dots as heterogeneous catalysts in transesterification for biodiesel generation.

C-dots are carbon-based nanomaterials with a zero-dimensional quasi-spherical structure [38]. They exhibit optical activity and fluorescence and are composed of an aromatic/aliphatic core with surface functional groups. C-dots usually have a size smaller than 10 nm, although there have been reports of C-dots as large as 60 nm [39]. C-dots can be classified into different types, such as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [40]. The specific type of C-dots depends on factors such as the hybridization of carbon, degree of carbonization, degree of crystallinity, type of crosslinking, and the presence of functional groups or polymer chains on the surface of the core (Fig. 1 ). This source provides a thorough examination of the intricate structural characteristics of several varieties of C-dots [41].

C-dots are synthesized using two primary methods: top-down and bottom-up synthesis. In the process of top-down synthesis (as shown in Fig. 2 , top), carbon structures of significant size are fragmented into carbon structures that are smaller than 10 nm. In contrast, in the process of bottom-up synthesis (as shown in Fig. 2, at the bottom), C-dots are formed by assembling tiny organic molecules. Traditional methods for synthesizing materials involve various chemical, physical, and electrochemical techniques [42]. These include chemical exfoliation, which is fast, scalable, efficient, and high-yield products but requires the use of toxic organic compounds [43,44].

Recent research has demonstrated that C-dots can be synthesized inexpensively and sustainably using affordable, environmentally friendly carbon sources [45,46]. These green synthetic processes are specifically engineered to enhance energy efficiency, employ safer chemicals and auxiliaries (some of which necessitate specialized development), and utilize renewable feedstocks. This results in a reduction in the use of dangerous chemical reactions and promotes a safer approach to chemistry to prevent accidents.

Moreover, the final products typically exhibit biodegradability and non-toxicity, resulting in decreased pollution during disposal [47]. Using natural organic sources has the extra advantage of being highly adaptable, as they can serve as feedstock for many materials, such as C-dots. The synthesis of C-dots from natural organic carbon sources using green technology offers a significant advantage in terms of both economic and sustainable production [48]. These synthetic methods are often straightforward, single-step or single-pot procedures that need several minutes to a few hours [49]. In addition, the majority of these methods for C-dot synthesis necessitate the use of uncomplicated, readily accessible, and commonly found laboratory apparatus and equipment, along with typical non-dangerous laboratory chemicals. Comprehensive evaluations on the production of C-dot from environmentally sustainable carbon sources can be accessed at this location [50].

Although C-dots possess inherent chemical and physical qualities that can be utilized, their traits often require enhancement for practical application. Typically, heteroatom doping or functionalization (as shown in Fig. 3 ) is used to adjust the physicochemical properties of C-dots. This process allows for the fine-tuning of these properties, resulting in C-dots that have desirable characteristics such as long-lasting chemical stability, easy dispersibility in water-based solutions, and enhanced catalytic activity. Doping is the process of introducing heteroatoms into the structure of carbon dots in a single step [51]. This operation is done to enhance their quantum yield, water solubility, and, most significantly, their luminous qualities. In the process of C-dot synthesis (as shown in Fig. 3), atoms of varied sizes and electronegativities, typically elements from Groups III, V, and VI, such as boron, nitrogen, phosphorous, and sulphur, are used to substitute certain carbon atoms in the core or surface functional groups. So far, nitrogen doping has been found to be the most efficient due to its similar atomic size to carbon, making it the most commonly used dopant [52].

Surface functionalization is accomplished through a series of steps in synthesis (see Fig. 3), in which modifiers (such as functional groups, polymers, biomolecules, ions, and metals) are connected to pre-synthesized C-dots through both covalent and non-covalent interactions. Fig. 1 illustrates that C-dots possess several oxygen and nitrogen-based functional groups on their surface, which can serve as potential sites for interaction with modifiers. The predominant surface modifiers for C-dot materials comprise boron, nitrogen, phosphorus, sulphur, the functional groups comprising nitrogen and oxygen, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups [53]. Infrequent modifiers encompass metal dopants such as manganese, copper zinc, and silicon [54].

C-dots are mainly recognized for their closely linked electron and optical transport properties, which are among their numerous advantageous physicochemical traits. C-dots, similar to other carbon-based nanomaterials with functional groups or defects, exhibit fluorescence [55,56]. When light is absorbed, electrons in the C-dot structure become energized and then transition to a lower energy state by emitting a photon. C-dots typically display emission that is dependent on excitation, meaning that the wavelength of their emission is determined by the wavelength of their excitation [57]. However, it is possible to generate C-dots that exhibit fluorescence that is independent of excitation. C-dots have demonstrated significant photoinduced electron transfer when exposed to light [58]. This process entails the movement of an energized electron from a giver to a receiver, resulting in the division of charges (the formation of an electrons-hole pair) or the inhibition of fluorescent (when the electron is moved from the giver to the receiver through a non-radiative route).

Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is a measure of how efficiently a fluorescent material produces light when exposed to light [59]. Electron population can be controlled by heteroatom doping and surface functionalization, which involves introducing additional electrons or holes to the structure of C-dots and electron-rich groups (such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino) to the surface of C-dots [60]. C-dots with up-conversion photoluminescence, a process where a substance transforms low-energy photons into high-energy photons, have also been created. In addition, C-dots exhibit exceptional stability and possess the capacity to maintain their colors even when exposed to ultraviolet and visible ligh [61]. This is a unique characteristic among fluorescent materials, as they often fade over time when subjected to prolonged radiation.

The numerous advantageous physicochemical properties of C-dots, as mentioned earlier, can be effectively utilized, rendering them highly desirable materials. For the production of biodiesel, the immobilized lipase could effectively catalyze the transesterification process of soybean oil and methanol. A biodiesel yield of 92.8% was achieved using this magnetic biocatalyst at a temperature of 40 °C through the three-step process of adding methanol in a swirling water bath [62]. To satisfy the demands of environmentally friendly and sustainable development, research into the creation of an immobilized lipase that is magnetically recyclable is crucial for the manufacturing of enzymatic biodiesel [63].

Nevertheless, it is the distinctive optical and electron transport capabilities possessed by these materials that render them remarkable multifunctional substances, hence stimulating recent scientific interest. Since their serendipitous finding in 2004, C-dots have been extensively employed in several industries (Fig. 4 A). These materials are mostly used in optics and have strong fluorescence, a high quantum yield, and impressive photostability. This enables them to improve the optical characteristics of other substances. C-dots can identify compounds by utilizing the photoinduced transfer of electrons fluorescence quenching in sensing applications. In biomedicine, they are not only used for sensing and imaging but also serve as excellent carriers due to their numerous surface functional groups, which can be modified with different moieties [64].

Additionally, C-dots play a role in catalysis by facilitating charge generation and separation, as well as enhancing electron conductivity to accelerate otherwise slow chemical reactions. According to Fig. 4B, there has been a consistent rise in scientific interest in C-dots since 2013. C-dots have mostly been utilized in anti-counterfeiting measures, light-emitting diodes for lasers, sensing applications, bio-imaging, and catalysis (Fig. 4C). Extensive evaluations of the uses of C-dots in optics, biomedicine, catalysis, and sensing are available in this source [65].

As previously mentioned, C-dots have gained recognition as very effective catalysts in recent years. This is due to their exceptional optical and electron transport properties, as well as their capacity to be modified through surface functionalization and heteroatom doping [66]. The presence of functional groups on the surface of C-dots gives them a high level of specificity and selectivity, which enhances their effectiveness as catalysts. Moreover, the incorporation of additional electrons or holes through heteroatom doping alters the internal electronic conditions of C-dots, hence enhancing their catalytic efficiency and promoting their interactions with reactants [67]. So far, C-dots have demonstrated exceptional photocatalytic performance and have been employed in the conversion of carbon dioxide, the splitting of solar water, and the destruction of organic molecules through both independent catalysis and co-catalysts with metals and semiconductors. The photocatalytic performance of a material is heavily influenced by its capacity to effectively separate and transport photogenerated electron-hole pairs [68–71]. Therefore, an efficient catalyst would be capable of generating electron-hole pairs efficiently and preventing their recombination for as long as possible. C-dots function as electron mediators and facilitate the movement of photoexcited electrons within their electron transfer network, so efficiently inhibiting electron-hole recombination [72]. This property makes them highly efficient photocatalysts. C-dots have demonstrated efficacy as electrocatalysts in many reactions, including hydrogen evolution, oxygen evolution, oxygen reduction, carbon dioxide reduction, and electrical energy storage when employed as electrode materials [73,74]. Although small in size, C-dots exhibit a significant specific surface area and facilitate fast electron transport. Electrocatalysis involves the reduction of the band gap size by C-dots, which enhances electron transportation efficiency and increases electric conductivity.

C-dots, which are carbon-based nanoparticles, have been suggested as potential heterogeneous nanocatalysts for producing biodiesel through transesterification [75,76]. These C-dots draw inspiration from other carbon-based nanoparticles such as carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers, carbon nanohorns, graphene nanoparticles, and graphene oxide nanoparticles [77]. Traditional carbon-based nanocatalysts have various drawbacks that hinder their widespread use and application, rendering them inappropriate for biodiesel production [78]. These limitations include high cost, complex synthesis processes, limited dispersibility, hydrophobic nature, vulnerability to surface defects, challenges in surface functionalization, and occasionally low conversion efficiency (Table 1 ). However, C-dots can be synthesized easily and inexpensively. They have a high dispersibility and are hydrophilic, which means they can easily mix with water [79]. Additionally, they can be easily modified and enhanced with other substances, making them well-suited as catalysts for heterogeneous transesterification.

C-dots were employed as co-catalysts in the in-situ transesterification process, as demonstrated in one of the initial instances. In-situ transesterification, often referred to as reactive extraction, is a process that entails the direct conversion of oil-bearing biomass, such as algae, into fatty acid methyl esters. This is achieved by putting the biomass into direct contact with an alcohol that contains a catalyst. Typically, esterification occurs in a homogenous phase with the aid of acidic catalysts such as p-toluene sulfonic acid, H2SO4, HF, H3PO4, and HCl. However, because these catalysts are harmful and corrosive liquid acids, using them is perilous. Thus, an approach to limit environmental harm and lower the cost of biodiesel could be acid heterogeneous catalysts.

An overview of recent studies on acid heterogeneous catalysts investigated in the esterification reaction to make biodiesel is provided. Numerous acid solids, including tin oxide, titanium oxide, zirconium oxide, sulfonic ion-exchange resin, sulfonated carbon-based catalyst, sulfonic-modified mesostructure silica, and hetero polyacids (HPAs), have been tested in the FFA esterification reaction; however, a major concern in the past year has been the use of aluminosilicates (mordenite, halloysite, and kaolinite), zirconium oxides, and cation-exchange resins as heterogeneous acid solids. Heterogeneous catalysts that can be used in both transesterification and esterification reactions are being investigated more and more, with the potential to be used at temperatures lower than those required for the esterification reaction [95]. Tangy et al. [96] employed C-dots as a co-catalyst of strontium oxide (SrO) to synthesize biodiesel from fatty acids derived directly from Chlorella vulgaris, a type of single-celled green microalgae with a lipid content of approximately 25%. A nanocomposite with catalytic activity was created by combining SrO with ultrafine C-dots, which were produced by sonicating polyethylene glycol. The nanocomposite consisted of 93 wt% SrO and 7 wt% C-dots. The nanocatalyst obtained was evenly distributed in methanol and subsequently added to C. vulgaris in chloroform. The system was after that exposed to microwave irradiation at approximately 60 ◦C for 2.5 min. The authors documented a lipid conversion efficiency of 97 wt%, surpassing that of SrO, the leading heterogeneous catalyst, when utilized as the only catalyst. By exclusively utilizing commercial SrO, the reaction time increases by over twofold, taking 6 min to attain comparable outcomes [96]. Borah et al. conducted an experiment where they combined graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with tungsten oxide (WO3) to transform waste cooking oil into biodiesel by utilizing methanol. Tungsten oxides, similar to SrO, are widely used as heterogeneous catalysts for transesterification. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized using the process of electrolysis, where citric acid was dissolved in NaOH, and graphitic rods were used as electrodes. The obtained graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were added to a mixture of tungsten disulfide and n-methyl pyrrolidone. The resulting mixture was then subjected to calcination to generate the WO3/GQD system. The catalyst exhibited a biodiesel production efficiency of 96.69% at a temperature of 70 ◦C.

Additionally, a catalyst loading value of 2 wt% was achieved at an oil-to-alcohol ratio of 1:6 after 3.5 h [97]. Compared to other catalysts based on tungsten oxide, this one demonstrates superior efficiency. It outperforms them in terms of biodiesel yields, even when subjected to equal temperatures, reaction periods, and catalyst loading values. Additionally, it maintains lower oil: alcohol ratios. The biodiesel produced met international norms. In a conclusive instance, Kalpana et al. acquired carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by subjecting exopolysaccharides derived from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 to hydrothermal treatment. These CQDs were then employed as co-catalysts in the transesterification of waste cooking oil with methanol, using both basic and acid catalysts. The introduction of these carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the reaction vessel resulted in enhanced biodiesel production when employed as co-catalysts for both sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid.

Nevertheless, the authors observed that CQDs exhibited superior performance when combined with the base, yielding 96.67%, compared to a yield of 87.27% when combined with the acid. The qualitative examination of the biodiesel produced through the transesterification of waste cooking oil with CQDs and NaOH indicates that it meets international criteria in terms of oxidative stability. The biofuel exhibited a high cetane number, which signifies excellent igniting characteristics [98]. C-dots, when employed as co-catalysts, exert a regulatory or augmenting effect on the catalytic efficiency of both heterogeneous and homogeneous nanocatalysts in the process of transesterification. C-dots synergistically enhance the performance of both types of traditional catalysts by expediting the transesterification process, decreasing the amount of catalyst required, augmenting the yield of the reaction, and enhancing the quality of biodiesel [99].

Recently, C-dots have been exclusively employed as catalysts in biodiesel manufacturing. Macina et al. conducted an early demonstration by synthesizing glycine-citric acid C-dots, which were subsequently employed as catalysts for the transesterification of canola oil with methanol. Macina et al. achieved a yield of 98.3 ± 0.2% at 150 ◦C within 3 h by using a catalyst loading of 1.0 wt% and an oil: methanol ratio of 1:18, which was found to be the most effective [100]. So far, two possible ways of functioning have been suggested (Fig. 5 ). Initially, the authors postulated that C-dots exhibit analogous behavior to homogeneous bases. During alkaline-catalyzed transesterification, a powerful base removes a proton from the short-chain alcohol, resulting in the formation of the alcohol's conjugate base. This conjugate base then proceeds to attack the carbonyl group of the oil. The presence of several nitrogen-containing organic functional groups, such as amine groups, on the surface of C-dots categorizes them as weak bases [101]. During transesterification, the amine surface functional groups remove a proton from the alcohol, resulting in the formation of the conjugate base. This conjugate base then proceeds to attack the oil.

Nevertheless, this process is accompanied by an energy barrier that can be surmounted in conditions of elevated temperatures and pressures [102]. Another possible mechanism suggests that the alcohol directly reacts with the oil to create a temporary intermediate zwitterion. The carboxylate groups on the C-dot surface aid in transferring a proton, so altering the balance of the reaction [103]. The authors postulated that transesterification occurred by both described methods, in which methanol directly reacted with canola oil and carboxylate groups on the C-dot surface aided a proton transfer, leading to subsequent typical transesterification. In their latest study, the authors have verified that carboxylate and amine groups present on the surface of C-dots serve as active catalytic sites [67]. They have also demonstrated that transesterification takes place through two different mechanisms, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (bottom). The specific mechanism employed depends on the relative abundance of each functional group on the C-dot surface. The presence of additional carboxylate groups in the C-dot nanocatalysts facilitates the acceptance of protons from the intermediate zwitterion that is generated when the short-chained alcohol reacts with the ester. This, in turn, enables the conventional base-catalyzed transesterification to occur. On the other hand, if amine groups predominantly occupy the C-dot surface, the nanocatalyst functions as a mild base that removes a proton from the alcohol, resulting in the formation of the conjugate base. This conjugate base then proceeds to attack the ester. It is important to acknowledge that an increase in the number of amine groups necessitates higher reaction temperatures in order to achieve a high level of biodiesel conversion during transesterification [104].

Nowdays researchers discovered that the presence of amine and carboxylate groups in similar quantities on the surface of C-dots leads to transesterification occurring through both pathways simultaneously, resulting in a modest process that regrettably reduces the yield of biodiesel. Medeiros et al. discovered that in order to catalyze the reaction, C-dots must possess a negative charge surface. When the surface of C-dots is positively charged, no conversion of biodiesel takes place [94].

C-dots exhibit exceptional efficacy as heterogeneous catalysts in the conversion of animal and plant oil and fats into biodiesel via the method of transesterification. Transesterification processes facilitated by C-dots demonstrate reduced reaction durations, decreased loading of catalyst values, and increased yields in comparison to those facilitated by traditional heterogeneous catalysts [105]. In addition, similar to other diverse nanocatalysts, C-dots necessitate high oil: alcohol molar ratios due to the fact that an excess of alcohol promotes the creation of methyl ester [106].

Diesel additives are compounds that can be introduced into petrol- or biodiesel in order to optimize fuel combustion, boost the general functioning of the engine, and potentially decrease the release of harmful emissions [96]. Multiple studies have demonstrated that C-dots are appropriate gasoline additives that can enhance and optimize engine performance fuel combustion and minimize harmful emissions [107]. For instance, Heidari-Maleni et al. incorporated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into a mixture of ethanol and biodiesel, which was subsequently utilized as fuel in an internal combustion engine (ICE). The researchers noted that the inclusion of GQDs resulted in a 12.42% boost in engine power and a 28.18% rise in engine torque. Additionally, specific fuel consumption was lowered by 14.35%.

Furthermore, carbon monoxide and unburned hydrocarbon emissions were both reduced by around 30% when compared to petrodiesel. A subsequent study was conducted to examine the impact of GQD concentration (measured in parts per million) on the performance of ethanol-biodiesel blends. They observed that the engine performance improved as the concentration of GQD grew [108]. Notably, the fuel containing 90 ppm GQDs exhibited the highest performance, which was in line with their expectations. When comparing the effects of adding 90 parts per million (ppm) of GQD to an ethanol-biodiesel blend with pure petrodiesel and a 10% biodiesel/petrodiesel mix, the power increased by 15.69% and 21.84%, respectively, and the torque increased by 15.39% and 16.26% respectively. When 90 ppm GQDs were added, specific fuel consumption reduced by 17.58% and 12.52%, unburned hydrocarbons decreased by 38.91% and 25.13%, and nitrogen oxide decreased by 1.54% and 0.25%, respectively, compared to petrodiesel and the 10% biodiesel.

Ettefaghi et al. achieved comparable outcomes in a separate experiment by introducing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into various mixtures of biodiesel, petrodiesel, and water [109]. The findings indicated a positive correlation between the concentration of GQD and the enhancement of engine power up to a threshold of 60 ppm. The inclusion of 60 ppm GQDs in the blend resulted in a 13.6% enhancement in engine performance, a 7.8% reduction in specific fuel consumption, and a 29.2% decrease in unburned hydrocarbon emissions compared to petrodiesel. The group effectively quantified the decrease in nitrogen oxides and soot emissions, resulting in a reduction of 3.8% and 33.7%, respectively. Nevertheless, it also resulted in a decline in engine performance and a rise in both smoke and unburned hydrocarbon discharges. This phenomenon arose due to the partial removal of oxygen-containing functional groups, increasing the surface area of graphene quantum dots (GQD).

The presence of oxygen-rich functional groups on the surface of C-dots rewards the oxygen deficiency in the burning chamber [110]. Furthermore, it was found that enhanced combustion leads to a decrease in hydrocarbons and soot emissions. High-quality biodiesel can be rapidly and effectively produced by utilizing C-dot nanocatalysts to catalyze transesterification. In addition, there is no necessity to eliminate the minute quantities of C-dots employed in transesterification [111]. Moreover, retaining them has a favorable impact on the overall efficacy of later biodiesel/petrodiesel mixtures, leading to enhanced engine efficiency and reduced harmful discharges.
The purpose of the research is to offer a guide for the development of an enhanced synthesis technique for C-dot base catalysts in the realm of biodiesel production.

C-dots have much promise for use in making biodiesel because they have a lot of unique benefits. Biomass sources are a more cost-effective, convenient, and secure option for obtaining carbon compared to other sources. The active surfaces of C-dots can be easily modified by introducing hydroxyl - and amine-rich containing functional groups. These modifications, in turn, influence the conditions of transesterification reactions, such as temperature and the ratio of oil to alcohol. In addition, C-dots exhibit exceptional specificity and conversion efficiency in the manufacture of biodiesel through transesterification. Using C-dots for transesterification is helpful because, unlike other heterogeneous metal catalysts, they do not react with the hydrocarbons with long chains in fatty acids when biodiesel is made or with the fatty acid methyl ester product that is made. C-dots have demonstrated a high level of recyclability, reaching a translation yield of 95% after undergoing five transesterification cycles. Transesterification catalyzed by C-dots often necessitates elevated temperatures and large ratios of oil to alcohol, in contrast to traditional metallic heterogeneous catalysts. The presence of carboxylate groups on the surface of C-dots enables the transfer of protons, which is necessary when the alcohol reacts with the oil and zwitterions are produced. When employed as co-catalysts, C-dots exert control over or amplify the catalytic capabilities of both homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts by falling the duration of the reaction, decreasing the amount of catalyst required, augmenting the production of biodiesel, and enhancing the quality of biodiesel.

Nevertheless, once the reaction concludes, C-dots might remain as a stabilizer to enhance the caliber of the resultant biodiesel and general engine functionality while concurrently diminishing the discharge of harmful soot, hydrocarbon and nitrogen oxide. Additionally, when used as additives, they have a positive impact on engine victory and ecological friendliness. Although there are several drawbacks to utilizing C-dots as catalysts in biodiesel production, such as the requirement for higher reaction conditions and ratios (oil: alcohol) compared to homogeneous catalysts, current research has demonstrated that these challenges can be successfully addressed. Nevertheless, the inherent benefits, such as rapid response times, minimal catalyst percentage values, and substantial yield, render them suitable as heterogeneous catalysts.

Jothi Vinoth Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mir Waqas Alam: Data curation. Manickam Selvaraj: Formal analysis. Hayfa Habes Almutairi: Formal analysis. Maryam Albuhulayqah: Formal analysis. Shima Sadaf: Resources. Merum Dhananjaya: Data curation. Sang Woo Joo: Data curation.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Jothi Vinoth Kumar is currently working as a Postdoctoral Researcher in the BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, Republic of Korea. He was born in Nathapattu village, Cuddalore district in Tamil Nadu, India, and his main research interest is in the area of Catalysis. He received his B.Sc degree from the Department of Chemistry, St. Josephs College of Arts and Sciences, affiliated to the Thiruvalluvar University in 2015 and M.Sc Degree from the Department of Chemistry, St. Josephs College of Arts and Sciences, Vellore, affiliated to Thiruvalluvar University in 2019. He worked as a Visiting Assistant Professor at the Department of Chemistry, Agni College of Technology, Chennai, from 07.11.2022 to 10.07.2023 and a Ph.D. degree from the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science & Technology in 2023. Research interests include facile green synthesis of metal oxide nanoparticles, carbon-based materials including carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, and their application towards the degradation of dyes, bioimaging, electrocatalysts, and food packaging.

Dr. Mir Waqas Alam is a distinguished scientist and Associate Professor in the Department of Physics at the College of Science, King Faisal University, Saudi Arabia since September 2015. He holds a Ph.D. in Nano and Functional Material Science from Toyama University, Japan. With a diverse research background, Dr. Alam has worked as a research scholar at Western Kentucky University, USA, and as a post-doctorate fellow at the National Institute of Material Science (NIMS), Japan. His expertise encompasses Nano and Functional Material Science, including areas such as Organic and Optoelectronic devices, Gas sensors Interface engineering, Organic Solar cells, Nanotechnology, Thin films, Crystals, Oxides, Green synthesis, Catalysis, Antibacterial, and Antioxidant. Motivated by a deep passion for advancing scientific knowledge, Dr. Mir Waqas Alam stands as a highly respected figure in the scientific community. His substantial contributions to the field underscore his commitment to pushing the boundaries of research and fostering advancements in science.

Prof. Manickam Selvaraj is a Physical Chemist who received his PhD award in 2003 from Anna University, India. In 2005, he got a prestigious Singapore Millennium Fellowship (SMF) and worked as a SMF Research Fellow at National University of Singapore. His research is Nanoporous Materials Chemistry in Catalysis, Controlled Drug Delivery, Energy and Environmental Science. He has published more than 200 premium research articles. He received and successfully completed more than 4 million USD research grants. His research group works with several huge research grants. He serves as the Editor, Editorial Board member and Primary/Adjudicate Reviewer in a variety of international journals.

Dr. Hayfa Habes Almutairi is a scientific researcher and Assistant Professor of Biochemistry in the Chemistry department at King Faisal University, Hofuf, Saudi Arabia. She holds a Doctor of Philosophy degree in Photochemical Sciences from Bowling Green State University, Ohio, USA, which she obtained in August 2018. Her doctoral research focused on the field of biochemistry, specifically investigating Protein Structure-Function Relationships. Prior to her Ph.D, she completed a Master of Science degree in Chemistry at Bowling Green State University in May 2013. Dr. Almutairi initiated her academic journey by earning a Bachelor's degree in Science and Education, majoring in Chemistry, from King Faisal University, Hofuf, KSA, in July 2008. With an extensive academic background and a commitment to advancing knowledge, Dr. Almutairi has made notable strides in her research endeavors. Dr. Almutairi is known for her significant contributions to various fields, including biochemistry, nanotechnology, biotechnology, and materials science. Her publications encompass a wide range of topics, including wound dressing materials, nanomaterials and their biological applications, antibacterial activities, energy storage applications, antioxidants, Antibiofilm and Antiproliferative Potential. Notably, her research has been published in reputable journals such as the ACS Chemical Biology, the Journal of Materials Research and Technology, Antibiotics, Biomedicines, and Polymers, among others. Dr. Almutairi's dedication to advancing science, conducting impactful research, and fostering a culture of learning and innovation makes her a valued asset in the academic community. Her multidisciplinary background, coupled with her leadership skills and commitment to excellence, positions her as a prominent figure in the field of biochemistry and beyond.

Maryam Albuhulayqah, a talented biomedical engineer, graduated from King Faisal University with a degree in Biomedical Engineering. Currently employed at Al-Hasa Hospital, her expertise lies at the intersection of biomedical engineering and healthcare. With a solid foundation in academic research and a commitment to innovation, Maryam is dedicated to advancing medical technology and improving patient care, embodying a passion for excellence in her field. With a rich background in academic research and a proven track record of leadership and management, she continues to make significant contributions to her field, demonstrating unwavering dedication and a relentless pursuit of excellence in all endeavors.

Dr. Shima Sadaf is currently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Electrical Engineering at King Faisal University (Kingdom of Saudi Arabia). She worked as a Graduate Teaching Research Assistant from August 2018 to October 2022 in the Department of Electrical Engineering at Qatar University. She was a Lecturer in the Department of Electrical & Electronics Engineering, Integral University, Lucknow (U.P.), India from July 2007 to October 2008. She has worked as an Electrical Design Engineer with Hyundai Engineering & Construction Co., Ltd. on the National Museum of Qatar Project from 2012 to 2018 and with ETA ASCON Group on various projects from 2009 to 2011 in Qatar. She completed her Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering (with Distinction and Gold Medal) from Qatar University, Doha, Qatar, in 2022. She received her M.Tech degree in Power Systems and Drives (with Honors) in 2007 and a B.Tech degree in Electrical Engineering in 2004 from Aligarh Muslim University (AMU), Aligarh (U.P.), India. She is also a Certified LEED AP BD+C and PMP® professional. She is an active member of IEEE, IEEE Industrial Electronics, IEEE Industrial Application, Young Professionals, and Women in Engineering Societies. She is also an active reviewer of various reputed international Transactions, Journals, and Conferences. Her research interests include power electronics converters, renewable energy sources, and Microgrid/Nanogrid applications.

Dr. Merum Dhananjaya is a Research Professor in Mechanical Engineering at Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. He completed his M. Sc. in 2012 and his Ph. D. in 2019 from Sri Venkateswara University, Tirupati, India. He worked as a Post-Doctoral Fellow at Yeungnam University from 2020 to 2021. He has published 47 research articles in prestigious international publications. He has made significant contributions to the synthesis and characterization of metal oxides in the form of nanopowders and thin films for their effective utilization in the field of electrochemical energy storage devices and sensor applications.

Prof. Sang Woo Joo received his B.S. from the School of Mechanical Engineering at Seoul National University in 1982, his M.S. in Mechanical Engineering from Seoul National University in 1984, and his Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, in 1989. He is a professor at the School of Mechanical Engineering at Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. He is a full member of the Engineering Department of the Korean Academy of Science and Technology. He has published more than 300 SCI papers on different research areas. His present work mainly focuses on microfluidics, nanobiotechnology, interfacial instabilities, phase changes, energy, and sensing materials.
